-I6EXWmF9Ys.title: -I6EXWmF9Ys
-I6EXWmF9Ys.transcript:
You could say it's the goal of art and science
to understand ourselves and our brain, our thinking is the most interesting aspect of
ourselves. The three reasons to reverse engineer the
brain; one, is to do a better job of fixing it. I quote one of the pioneers of the New Orleans
Plant for Parkinson's Disease in saying we're now treating the brain of circuitry, not as
a chemical soup, which drugs like SSRI, drugs just treat it like a soup of chemicals. It is in fact an elaborate network and if
we actually understand how that network works we can do a much better job of overcoming
problems and limitations, and for that matter we're all limited. Secondly, we can create more intelligent machines. We've done a fairly good job without actually
understanding how the human brain works, but now that we're beginning to get biologically
inspired methods, this is going to greatly accelerate AI. And thirdly, it'll provide more insight into
ourselves. We can't understand ourselves if we don't
understand how our brains work. Our identity, our consciousness, the concept
of free will is closely associated with the brain. Now it's not exactly the same thing. We can talk about how these philosophical
concepts relate to the brain, but you can't really have a discussion about consciousness,
free will, and identity without talking about the brain. Intelligence is also what we need to solve
all the major challenges of humanity and of the planet and so on. We need more intelligence. In fact, we won't be able to solve the major
problems that we have without more intelligence. So for all those reasons, it's important to
understand how the brain works, and it's really only very recently, I would say in the last
year, that we have enough information to actually make intelligent statements about intelligence
and how human intelligence works.

0BnZMeFtoAM.title: 0BnZMeFtoAM
0BnZMeFtoAM.transcript: Please welcome Uval Noah Harrari and Sir Steven Fry. >> Goodness me, I wasn't sure anyone would turn up. Isn't this rather exciting? Well, it's a great thrill for me to be with this um hero of mine really. Uh Uln Noah Harrari. I'm sure many of you would have read the book that uh catapulted his name into world fame, Sapiens, a remarkable history of our species. Uh which involved many many extraordinary insights and a kind of thrilling narrative of its own. But one of the things um Uval that uh I think many of us were impressed by was the way you showed that perhaps Sapiens was almost the wrong title. It wasn't that we were the wise uh humanity, but that we were storytelling humanity. That what separated us from the Neanderthalss and from uh other primates and and set us on our course was the fact that we told stories about ourselves and about the world as we apprehended it. Is that is that a fair summation? >> Yeah, absolutely. >> Yeah. And and would you say that this is both what gets us into trouble because a story is also another word for a lie and also what propels us into a a a stable future. Yeah, I think it's a double-edged sword in this regard, which becomes particularly important in the age of AI because for the first time in history, we encounter a better storyteller than we are. We took over the planet not because we are more intelligent than the other animals, but because we can cooperate better and we cooperate through storytelling. And you know, you have the obvious examples like religions, but my favorite example is money, which is probably the greatest story ever invented, ever told because it's the only one everybody believes or almost everybody. I mean, humans, no other animal on the planet knows that money even exists because it only exists in our imagination. Uh but now there is another thing on the planet that knows that money exists and that can maybe invent new kinds of money and new kinds of other stories. And what will happen to us when we have to deal you know it's not just with one better storyteller but with billions of better storytellers than us on this planet. That's the I think one of the biggest questions of our age. >> Yes. And you're talking of course about AI and it's recently come to light that one of the large language model corporations Anthropic um has revealed that its latest instance of of of Claude um was in a closed test was seen to blackmail some people in order to achieve uh a goal. And when we think about AI, we either get very uh dismissive of it. It's merely a parrot repeating probable um probable instances of human communication, a stochastic parrot as um as Emily Bender, the uh the the the computational linguist famously called it. It's uh every time it gives you a sentence no matter how intelligent it appears it is merely following a probabilistic route. >> The question is is it not the same with us? Exactly. When when I look at the way sentences are formed in my own mind like now when I'm talking with you I don't know how the sentence will end. I start saying something and the words just keep bubbling up in the mind and I you know as a public speaker it's something terrifying because I don't know if I will be able to complete the sentence. I'm not sure how what will be the next word. >> That's right. You you're talking and you're trying to make sense and suddenly you've come up with lettuce. Oh. Uh where did that happen? You know it's an unrolling carpet. inagmatic I think is the technical word for >> and the amazing thing with AI you can now ask the AI to show you exactly how it thinks. You can actually watch how the logic unfolds >> in the AI in a way which is very difficult for us to do with our own minds but we can now do I mean the AIs don't have minds at least as far as we know but we can see how the sentences the stories are being formed in their whatever we call it >> if we ask them to but the fact is I've spoken to people like Jeffrey Hinton and you know people who are called the god godfather of AI and they would say that the most disturbing fact about these current models is that nobody knows what's going on under the hood. They don't actually know how it's doing what it's doing. >> If we could know everything that's going on there and predict it, it wouldn't be AI. >> That's the point. I mean if if you have something that you can predict how it will behave, what decisions it will make, what ideas it will invent, by definition, this is not AI. This is just an automatic machine. >> The the way to um make that clearer perhaps is to recognize that AI now can beat just on your phone any chess player in the world. Um, and you obviously can't predict the moves it's going to make because if you could predict the moves it was going to make, you would beat it or it would be a stalemate every time. And you expand that to the whole of AI. You don't know what it's going to say or do, which is why it's a useful tool. If if if it could only match what we did, it would be not a tool. It would be like saying a digger, a JCB uh could only dig as much as a human with a spade. The point is we have these machines to outmatch what we can do. Yeah. >> Uh yeah, I think that that's the the main point. Uh both the main promise of AI is to be better than us >> at decision making in many fields that it would be able to invent things that we cannot think about. But that is also the the main threat. >> Yeah. that we cannot predict how it will behave and we cannot control it in advance. No matter how much we try to make it safe to align it with our aims, with our goals, I think the the the it really goes to the heart of what AI is. Yes. that usually we think about AI as an acronym for artificial intelligence. But there is no it's no longer artificial. >> If you think about what an artifact is, an artifact is something that we create and we control. >> Yeah. >> With each passing day, AI is becoming less and less artificial. Which is why I prefer to think about AI as an acronym for alien intelligence. >> Yeah, >> it is, you know, even the word in in a way a a true intelligence is never an artifact because an artifact is something that you create and control. Whereas intelligence is characterized by the ability to create new things. Yeah, you used a verb there very quickly, but it's worth expanding on it and that was align. And for those of us who are concerned, should we say about how AI is going, one of the main problems is what is known as the alignment problem. Um there's a simple way of looking at it which is an old philosophical thought experiment called the genie. Uh um if you imagine a genie who gives you a wish um and you love life and you're a empathetic person so you say to the genie can you really bring any wish true and they say yes you say in that case could you end all suffering and instantly all life on the planet is extinguished because to the genie they look at the world and all suffering comes in form of life all forms of life suffer smallest animals and us. That's the only place where suffering exists. Stones, as far as the genie is concerned, don't suffer. Maybe trees do. It's not sure. But if it gets rid of all life, it has solved our problem. And it bows and says, "There you are, master." Now, that's a very broad sense of the alignment problem. We asked a very stupid question. We didn't think it through. But the fact is yes we have to understand AI and alien but we really have to understand ourselves in order because we don't have shared ethical frameworks around the world. How do you encode dignity, love, sympathy, passion, joy, equality? We've only just learned if you like recently that women's and men's lives and dignities are equal and that people of different races are equal. We've only just discovered this and there are many other things we aren't sure about and how therefore can we expect the machines to be sure and therefore we ask them a question and it might do an equivalent of what the genie does. How do you see a solution to that? >> Well, one of the other key things about AI because it can learn and change by itself. Mhm. >> So whatever we teach it, we cannot be certain that it will always just comply by our instructions. Again, if it only does what we tell it to do, it's not really an AI. So when we think about the alignment problem or or how to educate AI to be benevolent and not harmful, it's a very problematic and imperfect analogy. But I think it's still useful to think about AI as a child, as the child of humanity. And what we know about educating children is that they never do what you tell them to do. They do what they see you do. >> If you tell a child, "Don't lie," and then the child observes you lying and cheating other people, it will copy your behavior, not follow your instructions. Now if we have you know the kind of people who are leading the AI revolution telling the AI don't lie but the AI observes them observes the world and sees them lying and cheating and manipulating it will do the same. Yeah. I mean if AI is developed not through a cooperative effort of humans who trust each other but it is developed through an arms race a competition. So again you can tell the AI as much as you like to be compassionate and to be benevolent but if it it it observes the world because AI learns from observation. >> Yeah. If it observes the world, it observes how humans behave towards each other. It observes how its own creators behave. And if they are uh uh ruthless, power-hungry competitors, it will also be ruthless and power- hungry. You cannot create a compassionate and trustworthy AI through an arms race. It just won't happen. >> Well, yes, you've said this. If you had said this in 1980 during what was known as the AI winter, we'd say, "Yes, we must plan and make sure this isn't happen." But it's already happening. There is an arms race. The very people who are spending the billions and billions are the same people, for example, Mark Zuckerberg and Meta who gave us the disaster of Facebook and social media and what it has done to the polity of the world and to the poverty of the world to essentially in this country as we know the rivers are polluted and contaminated. You wouldn't swim in any river in Britain because there's raw sewage being poured into it. Well, our children are breathing a cultural river which is similarly polluted and contaminated. And we all know this and Facebook knows this and Twitter and X know this, but they do nothing about it. And if you even mention guard rails and regulation, they scream communism. And Trump has just announced that he will ban individual states in America from regulating AI. There is an arms race. So everything you said is happening. So how can we come together to stop AI in the hands of corporate greed and national greed in this arms race? Whether it's China against America or it's one company against another, it's going in the wrong direction. >> Um yeah, it's moving very fast partly because there is also enormous positive potential of course you know in everything from healthcare >> to tackling the climate emergency. >> So we have to acknowledge there is also this enormous positive potential. The question is not how to stop the development of AI. It's how to make sure that it is used for good. And here I I think that the main problem is simply an issue of priority of what comes first. Humanity now faces two major challenges. On the one hand, we have the challenge of developing a super intelligent AI. Mhm. >> On the other hand, we have the challenge of how to rebuild trust between humans because trust all over the world, both between countries and also within countries is collapsing. Nobody is absolutely certain why it's happening, but everybody is able to observe it. I mean, maybe the last thing that let's say Republicans and Democrats in the US can agree on is that trust is collapsing. Yes. That they don't trust each other. They don't agree on any fact except that they don't trust each other. >> So this is the worst possible time for us to come together. We no longer believe in in global institutions like the United Nations, the WH institutions. >> Yes. >> I mean one of the explanations for the collapse of trust is that over thousands of years I mean humans have been amazing in building despite all the conflicts and tensions and so forth. You know, a 100,000 years ago, humans lived in tiny bands of hunter gatherers and could not trust anybody outside their band of 50 or 100 individuals. Now we have nations of hundreds of millions of people. We have a global trade network, a global scientific network with billions of people. So we are obviously quite good at building trust. But over thousands of years we have built trust uh through human communication and now in almost within almost every relationship there is a machine and algorithm and AI in between >> and we see a collapse of trust in humans whereas there is a rise of trust in algorithms and AIs. Again I I mentioned money as the greatest story ever told. So you see that people are losing trust in humanmade money like euros and dollars and pounds >> but they shift the trust >> from fiat to >> to cryptocurrencies and to an algorithm based money. >> So we have these two problems of the developing AI and rebuilding trust between humans and the question is which one we solve first which is the priority. Now you unfortunately you hear some of the smartest people in the world they say first we solve the AI problem >> and then with the help of AI we'll solve the trust problem >> and I think this is a very bad idea. If again if we develop AI through an arms race between humans who can't trust each other, there is absolutely no reason to expect that we'll be able to trust the AIS. I mean the big paradox is that when you talk with people like Mark Zuckerberg, like Elon Musk, they often say openly that they are also afraid of the dangerous potential of AI. They are not blind to it. They are not oblivious to it but they say that they are caught in this arms race that if I slow down my competitors will not slow down I can't trust them so I must move faster >> but then you ask them okay so you can't trust your human competitors do you think you'll be able to trust this super intelligent alien intelligent you're developing and the same people who just told you they can't trust other humans tell you, "Oh, but I think we'll be able to trust the alien AIS," which is almost insane. >> Yeah. Yeah. >> So, the the the right order of doing it is first solve the human trust problem, then together in a cooperative way, we can develop and educate trustworthy AIs. But unfortunately, we are doing the exact opposite. >> Yes. And there's, as you know, there's been for for decades the doomsday clock, which the the uh nuclear scientists um set midnight is Armageddon, the the end of everything. And it's been roughly at 89 seconds to midnight for the last few years. It's crept up over recent days for obvious reasons. But there's another metric that I've been studying recently called Poom. is the letter P which is probability brackets doom closed brackets. It's one used by people in the uh business. So you know the scientists in AI uh so for example Alicia Yudkowski who's the founder of the machine intelligence research institute in California sets P at 90 that's to say a 90% chance of human extinction through AI. uh Yan Lun who is the chief scientist for meta sets it at zero but then he is the chief scientist for meta so that's like a tobacco executive saying cancer no chance what are you talking about can't possibly happen but so I've worked out that roughly the lowest median is between 7 and a half and 10% of human catastrophe of of of an extinction order through AI if things are not controlled in the way you say they should be Now the chance of winning the lottery in this country is 0.0000000022%. [Music] Um so what you're saying here is that the chance of human extinction at 7.5% which is the lowest really amongst the the the current important scientists Nobel prize winners like Hinton and Assabis. Um if I would 7.5% is 3.4 4 million times greater than 000022%. So if I were to give you a lottery ticket and say this is a valid lottery ticket, the only difference is you are 3.4 million times more likely to win. You would take it and that's the odds we're playing with at a low rate. So let's look at the bad side of things. you, as we've said, we're going about it in the wrong order, as you've put it. Um, most people who understand the science say there is a very severe chance that humanity will be extinguished by this, a greater chance than by nuclear Armageddon, in fact. Um, or indeed climate change. Um, and humans are not in a position at the moment to trust each other and to establish guardrails to agree on how we should go forward. So, do you have a solution for us? You will I'm almost on my knees begging you at this point. I don't have children, so I can almost say I don't care, but I have lots of godchildren and I have lots of great nieces and great nephews, so I do care about what happens to our planet. And I'm sure you do, too. >> Again, I I think that it's very dangerous to think too much about, you know, kind of doomsday and extinction scenarios. they cause people to despair. Um, we do have what it takes to manage this revolution because we are creating it. You know, this this is not a dinosaurs being extinguished by an asteroid coming from outer space that they have no way of even understanding let alone controlling. This is a process which at least for now is under human control. In five years, in 10 years, we will have millions and even billions of AI agents taking more and more decisions, inventing more and more ideas. It will be a hybrid society. So, it will become more difficult. But we need to start with the re realization that this is a completely human-made >> uh uh danger. We have everything we need in order to manage it. The main missing ingredient is trust between human beings. And again, over tens of thousands of years, we have demonstrated that we are capable of building trust even on a global level. Yes. So, it's not beyond our capabilities. And it then goes back, you know, to the old questions of uh uh politics and ethics of how do you build trust between human beings and we need to think also about more kind of concrete and immediate uh uh questions. One of the biggest questions we will be facing in the next few years is um how to deal with these new AI agents. >> Yes. >> Do we consider them as persons? >> More and more people are entering relationships, personal relationships with AIS. more and more corporations and armies are giving AIS uh um control agency over important decisions. This is a not you know some kind of philosophical question. >> No, >> it's a completely like should AIs have the ability to open a bank account and manage a bank account? >> That's a very practical question. We are very close to the point when uh you can tell an AI go out there make money your goal is to make a billion dollars. >> Yeah. >> You know in in computer science very often the difficult question is how do you define the goal? >> Yeah. >> And one of the things about money it's a very easy goal to define. >> Yeah. >> Now an AI can make money in in many ways. It can hire its services to people to write their essays or books or whatever. Many many things it can do. It can earn money. It can then invest this money in the stock exchange. >> So do we as a society want to allow AI agents and then this is not a question for 50 years. This is a question for five years, maybe one year >> to open bank accounts and manage them in any way they see fit. >> Yeah. Um, in the US basically the legal system, they never thought about it, but there I'm not sure about the situation in the UK, but in the US there is an open legal path for AIS to be recognized as persons with rights. >> Yes, Pete Singer has written about this and others in in in the according to US law, corporations are persons and they have rights like freedom of speech. >> Yes. Now um so in the US you can incorporate an AI. Previously when you incorporated a corporation like Google or General Motors or whatever this was a fiction. >> Yeah. >> Because all the decision of Google were made by human beings. So okay legally Google is a person with rights but every decision of Google needs a human executive, accountant, engineer, lawyer to make the decision. >> Now this is no longer the case. >> You can incorporate an AI and the AI can make the decisions by itself. >> Yeah. The reason, one of the reasons that the US Supreme Court uh uh recognized corporations as persons is in order to make it possible for corporations to donate money to politicians. This was the Citizens United Supreme Court decision. >> Now, so imagine an AI that makes billions of dollars. It's maybe the richest person in the US in a few years will not be Elon Musk or Jeff Bezos or Mark Zuckerberg. It will be an AI. >> Yeah. And this AI has a freedom of speech which includes the right to donate money to politicians maybe on condition that they further advance rights for AI. >> Absolutely. And reduce guardrails and so forth. Yes. >> Now this is a completely realistic scenario. This is not like science. >> No. Absolutely. >> So and this is a question that we as a society >> need to decide. Do we acknowledge AIS as persons with rights? Now there are people who are already convinced that AIs because they have interaction with them have consciousness have feelings. So maybe in a few years the world is divided between countries that recognize AIs as rights and countries that don't. >> Absolutely. And I suppose for everyone in the room there's a consideration. I remember I gave a talk on AI back in 2015 in Hon and I I said that the way things are going uh there are certain jobs that people might not have. I said for example if you have a child who's studying to be a doctor that's fine but maybe a radiologist and a woman put her hand up very crossly and said my daughter's studying radiology and I said well you know imagine that every mamogram ever taken is available to an AI and it can examine thousands in a second and make a judgment on it. a radiologist is going to go out of work. Now, who's going to go out of work here? I don't know if the chief financial officer is president of Octopus, but uh I I have been sort of talking to some people who say that the first really highlevel job to be replaced by an AI completely will be a CFO. Um they can be so well everything they do >> it's already happened. It's news editors. >> One of the most important jobs in the 20th century even before was news editors. the editors of the newspapers, of the television, they are extremely powerful people who control the public conversation. >> But I think what what I would just end by saying to everybody is maybe don't concentrate on how efficient you are, on how brilliantly you complete a task because that's what AI can do, especially the agentic AI that you've been talking about. Con concentrate on what a wonderful human being you are, how kind you are, how courteous, how considerate, how you improve the life of those around you, which is very often the opposite of what efficient people do. And maybe that for for the time being at least is the secret that human exceptionalism will be how good we are as people. How how how much we make a room light up when we walk into it. How much pleasure we spread not how quickly we can complete a task because we'll never match the AI. So on that note at least I think we should end and I hope we haven't given too much terror to everybody. Um, I will always say that the thing about this technology is it's simultaneously thrilling and chilling. Um, and the thrilling parts may well cause us all to live much longer, happier lives. We can hope so. But you, Val, as always, an absolute joy speaking to you and thank you for >> Thank you so much. >> Thank you. Thank you everybody. Wonderful.

1M-zmCfwqus.title: 1M-zmCfwqus
1M-zmCfwqus.transcript: Imagine waking up just [music] a few years from now. You don't feel any different, but racing through your veins are millions of microscopic robots. They are patrolling your bloodstream, repairing your cells, and making their way straight into your brain. It sounds like pure science fiction, right? A dystopian movie pitch. But, according to one of the most accurate futurists on the planet, this isn't fiction. It is our immediate future. We are rapidly approaching what is known as the singularity, a point where biological intelligence and artificial intelligence will merge. The man behind this prediction theorizes that this monumental shift will be facilitated by nanobots. These are microscopic robots built from DNA strands or nanoscale materials designed to swim through our bloodstream and capillaries directly into our brains. >> [music] >> The goal? To connect your neocortex, the part of your brain responsible for complex higher-order thinking, >> [music] >> straight to the cloud. This will allow for a seamless integration of biological and non-biological intelligence. By the year 2045, human intelligence is predicted to expand [music] a staggering millionfold. But, we aren't waiting until 2045 for the invasion to begin. The timeline for these nanobots to enter our bodies for medical maintenance and brain-to-cloud connectivity is the 2030s. Are we stepping into an era where we become god-like beings, or are we engineering our own obsolescence? Today, we are breaking down Ray Kurzweil's terrifying, yet deeply fascinating plan for 2030, the technology that will make it happen, >> [music] >> and the dark risks that could end humanity as we know it. Strap in, because the future is arriving much faster than you [music] think. Part one, [music] the prophet of Silicon Valley. Enter Ray Kurzweil. He isn't some fringe science fiction writer typing away in a basement. Kurzweil is a world-renowned futurist, author, and a former researcher and engineer at Google. While at the tech giant, he specifically worked on machine learning and language processing, the exact foundational technologies driving today's massive AI boom. But, here is the number that should truly command your attention, 86%. Kurzweil claims a staggering 86% accuracy rate across 147 of his past technological predictions. He foresaw the explosive growth of the internet and the exact timeline of when a computer would beat a human at chess years before they happened. When Kurzweil speaks about the trajectory of human evolution, Silicon Valley's billionaires take notes. He first laid the groundwork for this entire timeline back in 2005 with the publication of his foundational book, The Singularity Is Near. For nearly two decades, critics called him crazy, but Kurzweil didn't back down. In 2024, he published his highly anticipated follow-up, The Singularity Is Nearer. In this new release, he fiercely maintains his core dates, arguing that the recent explosion in AI will directly accelerate the biology and nanotechnology needed for his 2030s nanobot predictions. So, what is the first major domino to fall? The year 2029. Kurzweil predicts that by 2029, artificial general intelligence, or AGI, will be achieved. He states that AI will successfully pass the Turing test, officially reaching human-level intelligence. We are talking about machines that can think, reason, and learn just like you and me. But, 2029 is merely the warm-up act, because once AI is as smart as we are, it will help us unlock the physical tech to hack our own biology, leading us right into the most terrifying and miraculous phase of his plan, the 2030s. Part two, the 2030s, [music] medical marvels and immortality. According to Kurzweil's timeline, this is the exact moment humanity crosses a threshold so profound it shatters the natural order of life and death. He predicts that in the early 2030s, science will achieve what is known as longevity escape velocity. In Kurzweil's own words, it means we will reach a point where every year of life we lose through aging, we get back from scientific progress. You are no longer marching toward an inevitable end. Technology is officially extending your life faster than time is passing. And the primary engine driving this miraculous immortality, nanobots. In scientific literature, nanorobotics typically refers to components scaled near a nanometer, that is, 10 to the power of -9 m, or ranging in size from 0.1 to 10 micrometers. In the mid to late 2030s, these microscopic machines are predicted to be deployed directly into human bloodstreams. But, before they ever touch your brain, they will serve as the ultimate internal medical team. Imagine an army of millions of microscopic robots constantly patrolling your [music] body. Their mission? To fight off infections, cure cancer, and actively reverse the biological aging process. You won't need to go to the doctor to find out you are sick. Your internal nanobots will have already diagnosed and neutralized the threat before you even felt a symptom. And it gets even crazier. Kurzweil envisions a level of dietary freedom that sounds like pure fantasy. He predicts that these nanobots will manage our metabolism so perfectly and efficiently that you will be able to eat whatever you want, as much as you want, while remaining completely fit and thin. The ultimate biological cheat code. But, curing cancer and letting you eat endless pizza is just the side hustle. The nanobots aren't just in your body to keep you alive. They are there to prepare you for the upgrade. And in the late 2030s, they are heading straight for your neocortex. Part three, the god-like expansion. Once the nanobots have secured your physical health, their primary directive begins. A primary function of these brain-based nanobots will be connecting the human neocortex directly to the cloud. The neocortex is the area of your brain responsible for higher-order thinking, conscious thought, and language. Imagine your mind no longer being limited by the physical space inside your skull. Kurzweil views this not as an AI takeover, but as an exponential expansion of humanity. By integrating biological and non-biological intelligence, he believes this synthesis will make us better, smarter, and ultimately more human. Kurzweil himself stated it bluntly, "We're going to expand the brain's neocortex and become more god-like." He promises that with this extra neocortex, "We're going to be funnier. We're going to be better at music. We're going to be sexier." But, what does a cloud-connected brain actually look like in your day-to-day life? Think about how you use your smartphone right now to look up an answer. Now, remove the phone, remove the typing, and remove the screen. Kurzweil and other futurists suggest nanobots could allow us to load knowledge directly into our brains via the bloodstream. Want to learn the French language? Just download it. And he isn't the only one saying this. Nicholas Negroponte, the founder of the MIT Media Lab, has echoed similar sentiments about nanobots being used for this exact type of advanced learning. But, the flow of data goes both ways. The brain-cloud connection won't just be for searching the internet. It will theoretically allow humans to back up their thoughts, memories, and consciousness, similar to how we back up a smartphone. If your biological body fails, the essence of who you are, your entire lived experience, is safely stored on a server, which brings us to the ultimate end game. Because if you can back up a human mind, what happens when you decide to turn it back on? Part four, 2045, the singularity and the digital afterlife. By the time we reach the 2040s, humanity will have transcended biology. With our nervous systems fully integrated with the cloud via nanobots, the next phase of Kurzweil's vision is the development of full immersion virtual reality. This is virtual reality generated directly from within your nervous system. You could live entirely alternate lives, feeling the wind and tasting the food in digital worlds completely indistinguishable from base reality. But, it's the physical world where things get truly uncanny. Remember those mind backups we talked about? Kurzweil predicts the arrival of afterlife technology, mind uploading, [music] in the 2040s. He believes that we will literally be able to upload the minds of our deceased loved ones and insert them into convincing android bodies. Death, as humanity has known it since the dawn of time, could become an optional experience. You could sit down and have a cup of coffee with a perfect replica of your late grandfather powered entirely by his backed-up memories, thoughts, and consciousness. All of this, the medical miracles, the cloud-connected neocortex, the digital resurrections, is building toward one final, world-shattering point in time, 2045. This is the projected date for the singularity. As Kurzweil himself declared, "I set the date for the singularity, representing a profound and disruptive transformation in human capability, as 2045." [music] In 2045, the singularity is officially reached. Non-biological intelligence will predominate our world, and human capability will be irreversibly transformed. We will be more machine than biology, the ultimate evolution. But, is this a utopian dream or an absolute nightmare? Because not everyone in the scientific community is buying what the prophet of Silicon [music] Valley is selling. In fact, some top experts believe this exact timeline will lead straight to [music] our ultimate destruction. Part five, the skeptics, [music] the divide, and the gray goo. As incredible as Kurzweil's 2030s timeline sounds, many neuroscientists are stepping up to hit the brakes. Take David Linden, a professor of neuroscience at Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine. He is a prominent skeptic of this entire vision, arguing that putting brain interfacing nanobots into humans by the 2030s is vastly premature given our current limited understanding of the human brain. Critics across tech and science forums are pointing out a massive blind spot, the difference between software and hardware. While software, like artificial intelligence, is advancing exponentially, hardware faces massive physical friction. Sure, Kurzweil's predictions about AI and large language models have seen massive validation in the 2020s, but nanorobotics? It remains largely experimental. In fact, critics argue that current nanotech is largely in the stone age compared to Kurzweil's grand visions. It currently lacks both the funding and the fundamental breakthroughs required to actually navigate the human body autonomously. Right now, real-world nanomedicine focuses mostly on passive nanoparticles. We use them for targeted drug delivery, like transporting chemotherapy to hard-to-reach tumors, rather than deploying active computing robots in the bloodstream. But, let's play devil's advocate. Let's say Kurzweil is right and the tech arrives right on time. That introduces a terrifying socioeconomic nightmare. Skeptics fear that if life-extending nano bots and cognitive enhancements actually do arrive, they will be monopolized by the ultra-rich. Imagine a world where billionaires don't just have more money than you, they are literally a different immortal species with god-like intelligence, drastically widening the gap between the wealthy and the general population. If you want to ensure you don't get left behind in this massive shift, you need to prepare right now. For that, we have created the AI Career Survival Guide. Don't wait for the AI wave to hit, ride it. Grab your copy from the description box now. And then, there is the ultimate, world-ending threat, the gray goo scenario. What happens if these microscopic machines are hacked? What if they malfunction? Some fear the existential threat of self-replicating nanobots turning into a weaponized swarm, potentially creating a biological or environmental plague that consumes everything in its path. If we put millions of connected robots inside our bodies, we aren't just hacking our health, we are turning ourselves into the ultimate biological attack surface. Despite all the criticisms and the terrifying potential consequences, Kurzweil isn't backing down. In his 2024 release, The Singularity Is Nearer, he maintains his core dates, 2029 for AGI and 2045 for the singularity. He firmly argues that the recent massive explosion in artificial intelligence will directly accelerate the biology and nanotechnology needed to make his 2030s nanobot predictions a reality. We are standing on the precipice of a profound and disruptive transformation in human capability. 1O-c1SUBCmE.transcript: The renowned futurist, Ray Kurzweil, [music] once made a chilling observation about human existence. Biology is a software process. Our bodies are made up of trillions of cells, each governed by this process. [music] You and I are walking around with outdated software, but here is the terrifying question no one is asking. What happens to society when the global elite get the ultimate software update and you are left completely locked out? In his groundbreaking book, Homo Deus, historian Yuval Noah Harari issues a brutal warning about our immediate future. He argues that as artificial intelligence scales, [music] it won't just replace human labor. It will create a massive, unprecedented, useless class of completely obsolete, un-upgraded humans. So, we may be facing in the 21st century a completely new kind of inequality, which we have never seen before in human history. On the one hand, the emergence of a new upgraded elite of superhumans enhanced by bioengineering and brain-computer interfaces and things like that. And on the other hand, a new massive useless class. A class that has no military >> [music] >> or economic usefulness and therefore also no political power. We are rapidly approaching the biological singularity, the exact moment we acquire the technological power to recompile our own genetic source code. For all of human history, extreme wealth could buy you comfort, but it couldn't buy you a different biology. A powerful king and a destitute peasant were ultimately bound by the exact same biological limitations. They aged the same and they died the same. But, the AI-driven technologies emerging today are about to permanently destroy that biological parity. Those who can afford these compounding technological advantages will effectively undergo a forced, rapid speciation event. They will utilize AI to continuously repair their cellular damage and maximize their intelligence, evolving from standard Homo sapiens into Homo Deus, god-humans. The gap [music] between this engineered elite and the rest of humanity will become so vast, Harari warns, it will rival the evolutionary divide between modern humans and Neanderthals. [music] Welcome to Technomics. Today, we are exposing the ultimate existential legacy of the AI revolution. We will reveal the exact artificial intelligence systems actively rewriting human DNA right now, the scientific plan to cure aging like a mechanical failure, and why humanity is about to irreversibly fracture into two entirely different species. The era of natural humanity is drawing to a close. Let's get into it. The proof of work, cracking the source code. You might think this biological singularity is some distant science fiction prophecy. It isn't. The assertion that biology is transitioning into a programmable substrate is an empirical reality established between 2023 and 2026. It stopped merely analyzing and mapping natural biological structures and started engineering entirely novel, highly functional biological machines from absolute scratch. We are witnessing the definitive end of the discovery phase of biology and entering the design phase. For decades, pharmaceutical research was trapped in a slow, brutal process of trial and error. That era is dead. Google DeepMind and Isomorphic Labs recently deployed systems like AlphaFold 3 and IsoDEE. IsoDEE doesn't just guess. It predicts small molecule binding affinities with an accuracy that completely exceeds the pharmaceutical industry's gold standard. It can identify entirely novel binding pockets on target proteins using nothing but the raw sequence as input. Designing medicine is no longer an archaic game of high-throughput chemical screening. It is a deterministic engineering discipline. Demis Hassabis, the CEO of Google DeepMind, profoundly stated, "I think of biology as an information processing system. At a fundamental level, nature's not random." He went on to emphasize that predicting single protein structures was just the starting line, declaring his ultimate 10-year goal is to computationally simulate an entire working virtual cell. But, simply mapping the pieces wasn't enough. We needed an AI that could fluently speak the generative language of biology. Enter Evolutionary Scales ESM3. ESM3 is a step toward making biology programmable. >> A computational beast trained on nearly 2.8 billion protein sequences representing the accumulated biological data of life on Earth. When researchers prompted this AI to create a new fluorescent protein, it hallucinated a functional biological machine 58% distant from [music] anything found in the natural world. Natural evolution, relying on random mutation, would require approximately 500 million years to achieve the structural divergence that ESM3 calculated in moments. Biology is no longer limited by the slow march of natural [music] time. It has been compressed into a computational prompt. And then comes the ultimate weapon, the ability to rewrite the human genome. Traditionally, scientists harvested natural CRISPR systems from bacteria to act as molecular scissors. But, in 2024, an AI built the world's first precision gene editor entirely from scratch, known as Open CRISPR 1. It is a fully synthetic editor, measuring an immense 400 mutations distant from its closest natural counterpart. Open CRISPR 1 achieved a 55.7% editing efficiency, completely [music] beating the natural baseline. And critically, it saw a massive 95% reduction in unintended off-target DNA cuts. And here is the truly terrifying part. The AI-generated molecule actually outperforms 4 billion years of nature's designs. [music] Ali Madani, the CEO of Profluent, cemented this reality by stating, "Our success points to a future where AI precisely designs what is needed to create a range of bespoke cures for disease. We are no longer limited by what nature provides. The tools required to edit the source code of human life are now being generated by algorithms." The engineering of aging, conquering the mechanical failure. With the fundamental source code of life finally cracked, the artificial intelligence revolution is now turning its sights on the ultimate human enemy, death. Historically, human aging was viewed through a rigid mathematical model called Gompertz's law, which dictated that our decline and mortality were inescapable, universal constants. But, the longevity science emerging right now has completely rejected this fatalistic view. Today, aging is no longer considered an immutable law of physics. Instead, it is definitively framed as a highly tractable mechanical engineering problem. To understand how we defeat it, you have to realize why we age in the first place. Human biology possesses incredibly sophisticated intrinsic repair systems, but their efficacy is strictly time-bound. Because the invisible force of Darwinian natural selection [music] drops off sharply after you pass on your genes, evolution only engineered your body to be good enough to carry you through your reproductive prime, roughly age 50 to 70. After that, your evolutionary warranty simply expires. Your systems accumulate noise, your cells degrade, and chronic inflammation takes over. But, here is the massive paradigm shift. Nature has already solved the engineering problem of extreme longevity. Species like the Greenland shark and the biologically immortal jellyfish exhibit negligible aging. Their mortality risk does not increase with time. Human aging is just an evolutionary oversight, and the evidence proves we can fix it. When a new human embryo is formed, its biological clock is effectively wiped clean, and all inherited aging markers are reset to absolute zero. The cellular software is fully capable of being rebooted. Leveraging Nobel Prize-winning discoveries like Yamanaka reprogramming factors, heavily funded biotech startups are now [music] actively engineering interventions to trigger this exact reset artificially within adult tissues. And they are using advanced AI discovery engines to do it, analyzing thousands of compounds and pathways to visually and molecularly force aged cells [music] to look younger. Renowned Harvard longevity researcher David Sinclair perfectly captures this paradigm shift. I look at the body like it's a a computer, it's software, and we can [music] reinstall the software. In my lab, we believe we've found a way to do that, and we see the evidence of that. So, when I see an old person walking down the street now, I don't think, "Oh, that person's just worn out, frail, going to die." I just think that's someone that needs a reset. And inside that person is a young person waiting to come out again. And the clinical evidence backing this up is already here. By applying short, controlled pulses of specific cellular reprogramming factors, researchers have already successfully reversed age-related vision loss like glaucoma and reduced systemic tissue fibrosis in animal models. The ultimate goal of this scientific paradigm is no longer just managing chronic decline or extending end-of-life care. The goal is the absolute compression of morbidity and the unlocking of decades of extended [music] youthful health span. Physical frailty and neurodegeneration will no longer be unavoidable ailments. They will be treated as downstream symptoms of an upstream failure preventable entirely through periodic cellular reprogramming. [music] The tools to make aging biologically tractable have been invented. The death of death is simply a matter of [music] computational execution. Morphological freedom and the designer reality. Merely living forever isn't the end game. The true biological singularity is absolute morphological freedom. Transhumanist philosophers argue that human nature isn't some sacred perfected state. It is simply bug-ridden code shaped by the blind, uncaring forces of random mutation. There is absolutely no moral imperative to remain strictly human if technology allows for transcendence into a superior, upgraded state. Imagine looking at your physical body not as a fixed reality, but as a fully customizable avatar. But the most immediate, highly consequential battleground for this philosophy isn't adult bodies. It is human reproduction. For the entirety of human history, the creation of new life was governed by the chaotic, random distribution of traits. Today, that ancient randomness is being actively dismantled in a profound transition from reproductive luck to reproductive math. While the public intensely debates the morality of physically editing genomes with CRISPR, the actual realization of designer babies has arrived quietly through a regulatory backdoor. The immediate threat is not surgical gene editing. It is AI-assisted polygenic embryo screening. You do not need to physically edit a genome if an algorithm can perfectly rank and select the ultimate, optimal one from a large batch. As of 2026, private genomics companies have fully commercialized this technology. They use artificial intelligence to aggregate the effects of thousands of genetic variants predicting highly complex traits before a child is even born. Parents are now using algorithms as a precise filter to selectively breed for projected adult height, physical appearance, and crucially, intelligence. Algorithms claim that simply selecting the smartest out of 10 viable embryos yields an average IQ gain of over six points. Startups like Helix Spectronomics are actively commercializing this technology utilizing vast genomic databases to generate highly predictive polygenic risk scores. By integrating advanced machine learning with non-invasive embryo biopsies, they have reduced fundamental human characteristics [music] to a consumer catalog. Critics warn this is a slippery slope into market-driven eugenics. But from a transhumanist perspective, failing to use artificial intelligence to optimize your offspring against polygenic disease and average cognition is not a moral victory. It is a profound dereliction of parental duty. The designer baby is no longer a science fiction trope. It is an accessible, highly desirable consumer product driving a massive wedge straight into human evolution. The existential end game. The emergence of a bioengineered, technologically superior elite completely dismantles the foundational myth of modern society, liberal humanism. We tolerate immense economic inequality today only because we assume a baseline of shared, organic humanity. We believe that a billionaire and a laborer possess minds of equal fundamental worth. But what happens when the elite literally possess genetically maximized intelligence, perfect emotional regulation, and centuries of prolonged health span? The core myth of human equality shatters permanently. As humanism dies, Harari warns it will be replaced by dataism, a new ideological paradigm where ultimate authority shifts away from human intuition and emotion straight into the hands of networked, omnipresent algorithms. In a society dominated by dataism, the unenhanced, organic masses will possess zero leverage, zero economic utility, and absolutely no political power. The terrifying reality of the useless class isn't that they will be actively hunted or eradicated through malice. The true horror is that they will be subjected to profound systemic indifference. To understand how this upgraded Homo Deus elite will ultimately treat the unupgraded remnants of humanity, Harari points out a highly accurate, terrifying indicator: how we currently treat animals. We don't exterminate livestock out of pure hatred, but our systemic indifference dictates their entire miserable existence. And the billionaires currently financing this technology aren't hiding the fact that they view baseline human biology as something to be conquered. Mark Zuckerberg has literally pledged billions to AI initiatives with the explicit goal to cure, prevent, or manage all diseases by the end of the century. Jeff Bezos has poured fortunes into cellular reprogramming startups like Altos Labs, warning that "Staving off death is a thing that you have to work at." Billionaire Peter Thiel bluntly summarized the ultimate transhumanist end game: "Evolution is a true account of nature, but I think we should try to escape it or transcend it." And for those who refuse or simply cannot afford to transcend, Elon Musk explicitly laid out the reality for the unupgraded: "We're going to have the choice of either being left behind and being effectively useless, or like a pet, you know, like a house cat." This isn't just one rogue billionaire. This is the unified consensus of the Silicon Valley elite. The gap between those who board the train of technological enhancement and those left behind will become entirely unbridgeable. We are literally witnessing the birth of the new Neanderthals. We are standing at the absolute precipice of our final evolutionary crossroads. The convergence of computational intelligence and molecular biology has successfully bypassed the harsh, slow constraints of Darwinian evolution. The ultimate existential legacy of the AI revolution will not be the mere automation of global industry. It will be the algorithmic divergence of the human species itself. The decisions we make in the immediate wake of these bio-algorithmic breakthroughs will determine if humanity manages a cooperative transition into its next phase or fractures permanently and violently along the fault lines of its own edited genetic code. The era of natural humanity is drawing to a close. The era of engineered existence has begun. If you are ready to navigate the biological singularity and understand the technological forces actively rewriting our future, you need the right data. Make sure to like, subscribe, and turn on notifications for Technomics. See you in the next one.

HMYVH-hBGWg.transcript: Information technology grows in an exponential manner. It's not linear. And our intuition is linear. When we walked through the savanna a thousand years ago we made linear predictions where that animal would be, and that worked fine. It's hardwired in our brains. But the pace of exponential growth is really what describes information technologies. And it's not just computation. There is a big difference between linear and exponential growth. If I take 30 steps linearly -- one, two, three, four, five -- I get to 30. If I take 30 steps exponentially -- two, four, eight, 16 -- I get to a billion. It makes a huge difference. And that really describes information technology. When I was a student at MIT, we all shared one computer that took up a whole building. The computer in your cellphone today is a million times cheaper, a million times smaller, a thousand times more powerful. That's a billion-fold increase in capability per dollar that we've actually experienced since I was a student. And we're going to do it again in the next 25 years. Information technology progresses through a series of S-curves where each one is a different paradigm. So people say, "What's going to happen when Moore's Law comes to an end?" Which will happen around 2020. We'll then go to the next paradigm. And Moore's Law was not the first paradigm to bring exponential growth to computing. The exponential growth of computing started decades before Gordon Moore was even born. And it doesn't just apply to computation. It's really any technology where we can measure the underlying information properties. Here we have 49 famous computers. I put them in a logarithmic graph. The logarithmic scale hides the scale of the increase, because this represents trillions-fold increase since the 1890 census. In 1950s they were shrinking vacuum tubes, making them smaller and smaller. They finally hit a wall; they couldn't shrink the vacuum tube any more and keep the vacuum. And that was the end of the shrinking of vacuum tubes, but it was not the end of the exponential growth of computing. We went to the fourth paradigm, transistors, and finally integrated circuits. When that comes to an end we'll go to the sixth paradigm; three-dimensional self-organizing molecular circuits. But what's even more amazing, really, than this fantastic scale of progress, is that -- look at how predictable this is. I mean this went through thick and thin, through war and peace, through boom times and recessions. The Great Depression made not a dent in this exponential progression. We'll see the same thing in the economic recession we're having now. At least the exponential growth of information technology capability will continue unabated. And I just updated these graphs. Because I had them through 2002 in my book, "The Singularity is Near." So we updated them, so I could present it here, to 2007. And I was asked, "Well aren't you nervous? Maybe it kind of didn't stay on this exponential progression." I was a little nervous because maybe the data wouldn't be right, but I've done this now for 30 years, and it has stayed on this exponential progression. Look at this graph here.You could buy one transistor for a dollar in 1968. You can buy half a billion today, and they are actually better, because they are faster. But look at how predictable this is. And I'd say this knowledge is over-fitting to past data. I've been making these forward-looking predictions for about 30 years. And the cost of a transistor cycle, which is a measure of the price performance of electronics, comes down about every year. That's a 50 percent deflation rate. And it's also true of other examples, like DNA data or brain data. But we more than make up for that. We actually ship more than twice as much of every form of information technology. We've had 18 percent growth in constant dollars in every form of information technology for the last half-century, despite the fact that you can get twice as much of it each year. This is a completely different example. This is not Moore's Law. The amount of DNA data we've sequenced has doubled every year. The cost has come down by half every year. And this has been a smooth progression since the beginning of the genome project. And halfway through the project, skeptics said, "Well, this is not working out. You're halfway through the genome project and you've finished one percent of the project." But that was really right on schedule. Because if you double one percent seven more times, which is exactly what happened, you get 100 percent. And the project was finished on time. Communication technologies: 50 different ways to measure this, the number of bits being moved around, the size of the Internet. But this has progressed at an exponential pace. This is deeply democratizing. I wrote, over 20 years ago in "The Age of Intelligent Machines," when the Soviet Union was going strong, that it would be swept away by this growth of decentralized communication. And we will have plenty of computation as we go through the 21st century to do things like simulate regions of the human brain. But where will we get the software? Some critics say, "Oh, well software is stuck in the mud." But we are learning more and more about the human brain. Spatial resolution of brain scanning is doubling every year. The amount of data we're getting about the brain is doubling every year. And we're showing that we can actually turn this data into working models and simulations of brain regions. There is about 20 regions of the brain that have been modeled, simulated and tested: the auditory cortex, regions of the visual cortex; cerebellum, where we do our skill formation; slices of the cerebral cortex, where we do our rational thinking. And all of this has fueled an increase, very smooth and predictable, of productivity. We've gone from 30 dollars to 130 dollars in constant dollars in the value of an average hour of human labor, fueled by this information technology. And we're all concerned about energy and the environment. Well this is a logarithmic graph. This represents a smooth doubling, every two years, of the amount of solar energy we're creating, particularly as we're now applying nanotechnology, a form of information technology, to solar panels. And we're only eight doublings away from it meeting 100 percent of our energy needs. And there is 10 thousand times more sunlight than we need. We ultimately will merge with this technology. It's already very close to us. When I was a student it was across campus, now it's in our pockets. What used to take up a building now fits in our pockets. What now fits in our pockets would fit in a blood cell in 25 years. And we will begin to actually deeply influence our health and our intelligence, as we get closer and closer to this technology. Based on that we are announcing, here at TED, in true TED tradition, Singularity University. It's a new university that's founded by Peter Diamandis, who is here in the audience, and myself. It's backed by NASA and Google, and other leaders in the high-tech and science community. And our goal was to assemble the leaders, both teachers and students, in these exponentially growing information technologies, and their application. But Larry Page made an impassioned speech at our organizing meeting, saying we should devote this study to actually addressing some of the major challenges facing humanity. And if we did that, then Google would back this. And so that's what we've done. The last third of the nine-week intensive summer session will be devoted to a group project to address some major challenge of humanity. Like for example, applying the Internet, which is now ubiquitous, in the rural areas of China or in Africa, to bringing health information to developing areas of the world. And these projects will continue past these sessions, using collaborative interactive communication. All the intellectual property that is created and taught will be online and available, and developed online in a collaborative fashion. Here is our founding meeting. But this is being announced today. It will be permanently headquartered in Silicon Valley, at the NASA Ames research center. There are different programs for graduate students, for executives at different companies. The first six tracks here -- artificial intelligence, advanced computing technologies, biotechnology, nanotechnology -- are the different core areas of information technology. Then we are going to apply them to the other areas, like energy, ecology, policy law and ethics, entrepreneurship, so that people can bring these new technologies to the world. So we're very appreciative of the support we've gotten from both the intellectual leaders, the high-tech leaders, particularly Google and NASA. This is an exciting new venture. And we invite you to participate. Thank you very much. (Applause)
