accelerating returns meaning it will double in power every year our biological thinking is is fixed go on to 2045 and the non-biological part of our intelligence will be a billion times more powerful than the biological part and that's such a profound change that we borrow this metaphor from physics and call it a singularity meaning we can't fully understand all the consequences you can imagine easily a lot of negative scenarios and you only have to look at sort of all the movies which are dystopian uh where the computers and robots have it in for us I have a somewhat different perspective and that I don't think it's going to be us versus them because I think we're going to merge with these Technologies and we're going to become enhanced with these Technologies and that's why we create these tools is to make ourselves greater we're the only species that seeks to change ourselves we basically create these tools to extend our own reach you a thousand years ago I couldn't reach that fruit that higher brand so I fashioned a tool to extend my physical reach we are going ethereal and I am a spiritual person so when Reay talks about Evolution as a spiritual process it's intriguing people use the word god which I think is a metaphor that means Unlimited in intelligence and love and creativity Beauty and so what happens in evolution well we see that entities get more intelligent more creative more beautiful becoming more Godlike so evolution is a spiritual process thank you guys for watching and please be sure to subscribe

PVXQUItNEDQ.title: PVXQUItNEDQ
PVXQUItNEDQ.transcript:
Let me tell you a story. It goes back 200 million years. It's a story of the neocortex, which means "new rind." So in these early mammals, because only mammals have a neocortex, rodent-like creatures. It was the size of a postage stamp and just as thin, and was a thin covering around their walnut-sized brain, but it was capable of a new type of thinking. Rather than the fixed behaviors that non-mammalian animals have, it could invent new behaviors. So a mouse is escaping a predator, its path is blocked, it'll try to invent a new solution. That may work, it may not, but if it does, it will remember that and have a new behavior, and that can actually spread virally through the rest of the community. Another mouse watching this could say, "Hey, that was pretty clever, going around that rock," and it could adopt a new behavior as well. Non-mammalian animals couldn't do any of those things. They had fixed behaviors. Now they could learn a new behavior but not in the course of one lifetime. In the course of maybe a thousand lifetimes, it could evolve a new fixed behavior. That was perfectly okay 200 million years ago. The environment changed very slowly. It could take 10,000 years for there to be a significant environmental change, and during that period of time it would evolve a new behavior. Now that went along fine, but then something happened. Sixty-five million years ago, there was a sudden, violent
change to the environment. We call it the Cretaceous extinction event. That's when the dinosaurs went extinct, that's when 75 percent of the animal and plant species went extinct, and that's when mammals overtook their ecological niche, and to anthropomorphize, biological evolution said, "Hmm, this neocortex is pretty good stuff," and it began to grow it. And mammals got bigger, their brains got bigger at an even faster pace, and the neocortex got bigger even faster than that and developed these distinctive ridges and folds basically to increase its surface area. If you took the human neocortex and stretched it out, it's about the size of a table napkin, and it's still a thin structure. It's about the thickness of a table napkin. But it has so many convolutions and ridges it's now 80 percent of our brain, and that's where we do our thinking, and it's the great sublimator. We still have that old brain that provides our basic drives and motivations, but I may have a drive for conquest, and that'll be sublimated by the neocortex into writing a poem or inventing an app or giving a TED Talk, and it's really the neocortex that's where the action is. Fifty years ago, I wrote a paper describing how I thought the brain worked, and I described it as a series of modules. Each module could do things with a pattern. It could learn a pattern. It could remember a pattern. It could implement a pattern. And these modules were organized in hierarchies, and we created that hierarchy with our own thinking. And there was actually very little to go on 50 years ago. It led me to meet President Johnson. I've been thinking about this for 50 years, and a year and a half ago I came out with the book "How To Create A Mind," which has the same thesis, but now there's a plethora of evidence. The amount of data we're getting about the brain from neuroscience is doubling every year. Spatial resolution of brainscanning of all types is doubling every year. We can now see inside a living brain and see individual interneural connections connecting in real time, firing in real time. We can see your brain create your thoughts. We can see your thoughts create your brain, which is really key to how it works. So let me describe briefly how it works. I've actually counted these modules. We have about 300 million of them, and we create them in these hierarchies. I'll give you a simple example. I've got a bunch of modules that can recognize the crossbar to a capital A, and that's all they care about. A beautiful song can play, a pretty girl could walk by, they don't care, but they see
a crossbar to a capital A, they get very excited and they say "crossbar," and they put out a high probability on their output axon. That goes to the next level, and these layers are organized in conceptual levels. Each is more abstract than the next one, so the next one might say "capital A." That goes up to a higher
level that might say "Apple." Information flows down also. If the apple recognizer has seen A-P-P-L, it'll think to itself, "Hmm, I
think an E is probably likely," and it'll send a signal down to all the E recognizers saying, "Be on the lookout for an E, I think one might be coming." The E recognizers will lower their threshold and they see some sloppy
thing, could be an E. Ordinarily you wouldn't think so, but we're expecting an E, it's good enough, and yeah, I've seen an E, and then apple says, "Yeah, I've seen an Apple." Go up another five levels, and you're now at a pretty high level of this hierarchy, and stretch down into the different senses, and you may have a module
that sees a certain fabric, hears a certain voice quality,
smells a certain perfume, and will say, "My wife has entered the room." Go up another 10 levels, and now you're at a very high level. You're probably in the frontal cortex, and you'll have modules that say, "That was ironic. That's funny. She's pretty." You might think that those are more sophisticated, but actually what's more complicated is the hierarchy beneath them. There was a 16-year-old girl, she had brain surgery, and she was conscious because the surgeons wanted to talk to her. You can do that because there's no pain receptors in the brain. And whenever they stimulated particular, very small points on her neocortex, shown here in red, she would laugh. So at first they thought they were triggering some kind of laugh reflex, but no, they quickly realized they had found the points in her neocortex that detect humor, and she just found everything hilarious whenever they stimulated these points. "You guys are so funny just standing around," was the typical comment, and they weren't funny, not while doing surgery. So how are we doing today? Well, computers are actually beginning to master human language with techniques that are similar to the neocortex. I actually described the algorithm, which is similar to something called a hierarchical hidden Markov model, something I've worked on since the '90s. "Jeopardy" is a very broad natural language game, and Watson got a higher score than the best two players combined. It got this query correct: "A long, tiresome speech delivered by a frothy pie topping," and it quickly responded,
"What is a meringue harangue?" And Jennings and the other guy didn't get that. It's a pretty sophisticated example of computers actually understanding human language, and it actually got its knowledge by reading Wikipedia and several other encyclopedias. Five to 10 years from now, search engines will actually be based on not just looking for combinations of words and links but actually understanding, reading for understanding the billions of pages on the web and in books. So you'll be walking along, and Google will pop up and say, "You know, Mary, you expressed concern to me a month ago that your glutathione supplement wasn't getting past the blood-brain barrier. Well, new research just came out 13 seconds ago that shows a whole new approach to that and a new way to take glutathione. Let me summarize it for you." Twenty years from now, we'll have nanobots, because another exponential trend is the shrinking of technology. They'll go into our brain through the capillaries and basically connect our neocortex to a synthetic neocortex in the cloud providing an extension of our neocortex. Now today, I mean, you have a computer in your phone, but if you need 10,000 computers for a few seconds to do a complex search, you can access that for a second or two in the cloud. In the 2030s, if you need some extra neocortex, you'll be able to connect to that in the cloud directly from your brain. So I'm walking along and I say, "Oh, there's Chris Anderson. He's coming my way. I'd better think of something clever to say. I've got three seconds. My 300 million modules in my neocortex isn't going to cut it. I need a billion more." I'll be able to access that in the cloud. And our thinking, then, will be a hybrid of biological and non-biological thinking, but the non-biological portion is subject to my law of accelerating returns. It will grow exponentially. And remember what happens the last time we expanded our neocortex? That was two million years ago when we became humanoids and developed these large foreheads. Other primates have a slanted brow. They don't have the frontal cortex. But the frontal cortex is not
really qualitatively different. It's a quantitative expansion of neocortex, but that additional quantity of thinking was the enabling factor for us to take a qualitative leap and invent language and art and science and technology and TED conferences. No other species has done that. And so, over the next few decades, we're going to do it again. We're going to again expand our neocortex, only this time we won't be limited by a fixed architecture of enclosure. It'll be expanded without limit. That additional quantity will again be the enabling factor for another qualitative leap in culture and technology. Thank you very much. (Applause)

Q0tKnUoMq-g.title: Q0tKnUoMq-g
Q0tKnUoMq-g.transcript:
Welcome, everyone. I'm Agustin Rayo. I'm the Kenan Sahin Dean of
the School of Humanities, Arts, and Social Sciences, or
SHASS, as we call it, at MIT. And it is my privilege to
welcome you to the Robert A. Muh Award ceremony. Thanks for being here. The Robert A. Muh Award was
established in the year 2000, which was the 50th
anniversary of SHASS. Bob Muh, who created the
award, class of 1959, aimed to honor MIT graduates in
the fields of humanities, arts, and social sciences
who have gone on to extraordinary
achievements. Bob is a co-founder of Sutter
Securities, Incorporated in San Francisco. He has served on a number of
SHASS visiting committees. And he is a life member
emeritus of the MIT Corporation. We are so grateful for all
that you have done for MIT. [APPLAUSE] So-- Bob is here, as is his wife
Berit and their daughter Carrie, who is herself an MIT graduate. So welcome to all three of you. [APPLAUSE] I would also like to welcome
MIT Corporation Chair Mark Gorenberg and several other
members of the Corporation who are here today. Thank you so much
for being here. Our president, Sally Kornbluth,
is not able to be here and she sends her regrets. I also want to recognize Philip
Khoury, who I cannot see now-- oh, there he is. So until very recently,
he was our Vice Provost for the Arts, a position
he held for about 20 years. And before then he had my
role and he worked with Bob on setting up this award. So thank you, Philip, and
thank you for being here. [APPLAUSE] Today we recognize Ray Kurzweil. He received his
undergraduate degree at MIT in computer science
and literature in 1970. And he is one of the most
prolific thinkers of our time. The Wall Street Journal called
him the restless genius. Forbes called him the
ultimate thinking machine. Inc. magazine described
him as the rightful heir to Thomas Edison. He was recently named
chief AI officer of at Beyond Imagination,
which is a humanoid robotics company that he co-founded. Before then, he was at Google. Ray is a visionary and futurist. There is perhaps no one
with a better understanding of technology, the pace
of its advancement, and its impact on humanity. His law of accelerating
returns has allowed him to make
astonishingly accurate predictions about the future
of technological innovation, including accurate predictions
about the rapid acceleration of AI technology and
its impact on our lives. Ray is an inventor. He has created many things that
make the world a better place. He, for example, designed
the first reading machine for the blind, a technology
that has had many other uses. And he designed
the Kurzweil 250, which is a revolutionary
digital synthesizer. Ray is a writer who has five
best-selling books, including his latest, The
Singularity Is Near. And he is currently writing
Artificial General Intelligence is Here, which I very much
look forward to reading. He has received
countless honors, including 21 honorary
degrees, the National Medal of Technology. He's a member of the National
Inventors Hall of Fame. He has been an honoree
of three presidents, and he has a Grammy for
Outstanding Achievement to Music Technology. MIT has been benefited
from his service to the Corporation
between 2005 and 2012, and Ray continues to
be actively involved. He has built his life and
his career on the belief that ideas change the world
and change it for the better. As he says, "There is a
solution to every problem, we just have to think of it. I would now like to
invite Bob and Ray to the stage for the
presentation of the 2025 Robert A. Muh Alumni Award
to Ray Kurzweil. [APPLAUSE] It's beautiful. [APPLAUSE] Ray the stage is yours. Thank you. Well, it's beautiful to be here. It's a brand new
building devoted to music, which you didn't
have when I was here. I come from a musical family,
so it's really compelling to be here. You should hear me very well,
because the acoustics here is pretty amazing. So thank you, Agustin, for
your kind introduction. That was actually quite nice. I'll ask you to give me an
introduction at other events. I'm truly honored to
receive this award. And I'm delighted to have Bob
and Berit Muh here, as well as her daughter, Carrie. Your long-standing commitment
to our alma mater is inspiring, and I'm grateful
to be recognized by you and the School of
Humanities, Arts, and Social sciences. So I actually majored in
computer science, which was a brand new
field and I actually did not get its own number. It has the number 6, 1. But I also majored
in creative writing. And I've ' so. I've actually
continued both careers. MIT has played a major
role in my personal and professional
life over the years. I have c fond memories of being
a student here in the 1960s, which is a long time ago. It's like 60 years ago. Having MIT Press
published my first book, The Age of Intelligent
Machines, giving lectures to many excellent
conferences and classes, serving as a member of the
Corporation for seven years. I see a couple of people
from the corporation here. Early this year,
I had the pleasure of talking to the
corporation in Washington about the future of artificial
intelligence and education. If I think back to when I
went to MIT, as I mentioned, was 60 years ago, It feels like it's
part of my life now. It doesn't seem that long ago. I can remember it
seems like yesterday, but if I think back to my last
year of high school, which was 61 years ago, it seems
like a different lifetime. So going to MIT, that was really
the beginning of my adult life. And today, I'm
going to be talking about reinventing intelligence
and the story of how I came to MIT-- dedicated 63 years to
studying and developing AI. My involvement with
AI goes back 63 years. If I told people I
was involved with AI, they hadn't heard of it. In fact, they hadn't even
heard of computers back then. So, anyway, I'd like
to start with a poem-- actually, a funny poem. "A squirrel named Nuts,
with a twitch in his tail-- very important
looking, that twitch-- discovered a peanut. not a peanut, mind
you, but the peanut-- round, fat, glorious left just
so by some two-legged giant, probably forgetful. He chirped a small explosion
of glee or possibly gas, it's hard to tell with
squirrels and grabbed it with paws so quick they were
almost invisible, like Monday morning motivation. Then horror, A blue jay, a
feathered thief with eyes like polished disdain, swooped. Nuts, our hero, with the
aforementioned twitching tail did not flinch. He juked. He jibed. He became a flurry blur
of anti-jay acrobatics." Well, it goes on. And this poem was
written by Gemini 2.5. I simply asked it to
compose a funny poem in the voice of E. E. Cummings. And I've actually been
doing this every year, and it did not work a year ago. I asked a number of different
large language models to create a funny poem. It had some kind of thing
that wasn't expected, but it really wasn't funny. Now, what is funny? It's really hard to describe. But I actually did this
five times with Gemini 2.5. Each time it was funny
in different ways. So defining humor
is not so easy. It requires a deep
understanding of language, what people expect to hear. So to be able to tap
into humor is perhaps one of the most
basic human traits. We cannot define it,
yet we respond to it. Now, there are several
books which I've looked at and what humor is and how they
are difficult to understand. I will say that these
books are not funny. It's compelling. The large language
model has actually begun to understand humor. What we will respond
to as being funny. It's a big step and
understanding the emotions that people have. The one key that I would
like to emphasize this is just one of the
many advances in AI that the last year has shown. This wasn't true a year ago. Now it's true. And the key thing
that this shows that is the rate of progress. The next year will show
even more advancement and this will continue
year after year. People do not appreciate
that the rate of progress is accelerating. It's hard to hear me give a
speech without using the word accelerating. Let me come back to that. So this is me at
three years old. And I would spend a
lot of time looking at mechanical and
electronic objects, which is what I have in
my hand and trying to understand how they worked. So this was the formative
stage of my inventing career. My parents taught me
the power of ideas can really change the world. That was the religion
that I grew up in. No matter what
quandaries we face-- business problems,
health issues, relationship difficulties-- as was mentioned
before, there's an idea that can enable us to prevail. And it's up to us
to find that idea. And it's personal. You, Ray, or you, whatever your
name is, can find that idea. Now, both my family and
my wife, Sonia's family had fled the Nazis in Europe. Their stories seemed
completely impossible. They survived despite
all odds against them. They gave me a
genuine appreciation for how the arts
and the innovation can bring people together. So this is me with my parents. My father was a musician. He's actually preparing there
to conduct an orchestra. My mother was an artist. It was actually his music
that saved his life. In 1937, when he was just 25, he
gave an orchestral performance of Brahms for an
international audience. And in the crowd that night
was Gertrude Sumner Ely, a friend of Eleanor Roosevelt. After the concert, she
handed my father her card, said reach out if you
ever need anything. So a year later, he
actually kept the card, in 1938 when he was forced to
flee Austria, he did contact her and she sponsored him, which was
necessary for saving his life. My mother always encouraged
me to pursue my ideas. She was difficult in her last
stage because of a problem she had. But she-- when I
was 10, 12, we would take these long
walks in the woods and I would tell her my ideas. And no matter how fantastical
they were she believed them. Other parents might
have simply chuckled, they might have pretended
they believed them, but she actually
believed my ideas. And that actually
gave me my confidence. And I think confidence is
important to succeeding. Her father also had
the rare opportunity to travel back to
Austria after the war. And he was actually interested
in how people were serving him when they were trying to kill
him when he tried to leave. He was actually
given the opportunity to handle Leonardo da
Vinci's original manuscripts with his own hands. He described the
experience with reverence, as if he had touched the word-- the work of God. This was the religion
that I was raised with-- devotion to human creativity
and the power of ideas. My great grandmother on my
mother's side and other members of my family started the
[? Sterne ?] [? Schuler ?] in 1858. This was one of the very
first schools in Europe that provided higher
education for girls. So if you were trying
to get an education-- first of all, it's lucky if
you got any education as a girl in 19th century Europe. Her daughter, my
grandmother, took it over. It was one of the
first women in Europe to receive a PhD in chemistry. And the school actually went
from kindergarten up to 14th grade. If you were able to get an
education at all in Europe, it generally went
through ninth grade. So later my grandmother
wrote a book called, One Life is not Enough. It's actually a
good title for one of my books on life extension. And she showed it to me
when I was seven years old. Now, the book itself didn't
interest me at the time, although it did later. But the manual typewriter
she had written it on was amazing to me. It was a magical machine. It could actually turn
a blank piece of paper into something that looked
like it came from a book. I studied every
mechanism inside it. That was my first inspiration
to become an Inventor. Other kids were wondering
what they wanted to be-- teacher, policeman. I always knew that I would be
an Inventor since age of seven. I built a go-kart that I rode
around in my neighborhood, searching for broken
bicycles, broken radios that I could bring home. I took them apart. I was sure if I
could just figure out how I could put these things
back together in the right way, I could solve any problem. And I really had this idea. Didn't how to put them together,
but I could solve any problem if I could figure that out. I remember talking to
some much older girls, I think they were about 10. I told them that I was
going to build machines. They could do anything like fly,
see through walls, and so on. They said something about me
having quite an imagination. So the first time I ever worked
on a computer was when I was 12. Back in 1960, there were
only about 12 computers in all of New York City. People had not heard
of computers, let alone artificial intelligence. And I got access to one
during the overnight shift at Flower Fifth Avenue
Hospital in Spanish Harlem. Not a great deal to walk around
in Spanish Harlem in the middle of the night, but that's-- I had the 12 o'clock
to 8:00 AM shift. I had a job programming
statistical test to evaluate Head Start, its
early education program. This was the beginning
of Head Start, which is still a major program. After I entered the data, the
front light of the computer, which you can actually see here,
would dim and glow like a dance as it was considering
the information. And I remember
thinking about it. It just took several minutes. It looked like it
was deep in thought. And I was immediately
struck by the relationship between computers
and intelligence. Now, keep in mind, there was no
internet, no email, no Google. The only way to explore
anything was to go to a library. The main way to
research something was to have a bag of quarters so
that you could feed the copier to make copies of research
books that you were not able to check out. That's when I learned about a
new, obscure academic field, which has its origins here
called artificial intelligence. It had only gotten its name
six years earlier, in 1956, at a conference at
Dartmouth College. The name was created by John
McCarthy and Marvin Minsky. You must remember Marvin Minsky. And he was about 29. I noticed that Minsky
was not far away at MIT and I was in Queens, New York. So 1962, when I was
14, I wrote Minsky that I would like to see him
and talk about this new field. I still have a copy
of that letter. He wrote back quickly,
invited me to visit him, so I came to Cambridge. I figured I would meet him
for maybe half an hour, but he actually
spent all day with me like he had nothing
more important to do. He invited me to come to
MIT and he became my mentor for 50 years. I'm writing an autobiography
and I say a lot about my interactions with him. I chose MIT because
of Marvin Minsky. And because MIT was so
advanced, it actually had its own computer. I remember when I was a junior
in 1968, it had an IBM 7094. My closest friend at
MIT was Aaron Kleiner. He's actually here. He's actually wearing a suit,
so it might be hard to see him, but, yeah, he looked
pretty good 60 years ago. [LAUGHTER] We worked together ever
since we graduated. And my other brothers at
Tau Epsilon Phi, that's TEP, used to call me the phantom
because one minute I'd be there and the next I'd be gone for
days working on my computer projects. Aaron likes to tell
the story of cramming the night before a physics
exam when I strolled in, just remembered, oh, the
exam is tomorrow. So I borrowed his book
for an hour or two, I tried to ace the exam, I
won't tell you what happened, but I have to admit that
I didn't actually take my coursework very seriously. I was very concerned with
my projects, the impact that I could have, and this
led to recurring nightmares about desperately searching
for my lost textbooks and class notes, which I still
have to this day. [LAUGHTER] Up until the 1970s, optical
character recognition programs could only has a single font. That made them
pretty limited when it came to scanning
real world documents. So my goal was to
build a computer that could recognize any font. And we pulled it off. The funny thing is,
we didn't really what to do with it at first. Like a lot of
clever software, it was a solution in
search of a problem. So I happened to actually
sit next to a blind gentleman on a plane. He had no trouble
communicating or traveling. He told me there's really
nothing he can't do. But he said, actually, there's
one thing that's a challenge and it's reading ordinary
printed material. Only 3% of the books
are put into Braille. So that became our focus. That was the problem
we'd been looking for. I realized we could use our Omni
Font or any font, OCR, to help overcome the
barrier of accessing printed material for the blind. Of course, that meant we had
to invent a few other things. A flatbed scanner,
which didn't exist. Text-to-speech synthesis,
which also wasn't around. And at that point, I didn't
much about blindness. So I reached out to the
many blindness organizations for their guidance. It was the National
Federation of the Blind, which you can see here. And that's actually the reading
machine when we introduced it. And they replied with concrete
ideas and immediate enthusiasm. They're an organization for
blind people run by blind people with over 50,000 members. It's the largest organization
of its kind worldwide. I'm happy to have Anil
Lewis here from the National Federation of the Blind. I worked with the National
Federation of Blind to hire eight blind
engineers, which worked on every aspect
of the reading machine, including helping design
the machine's user controls. So this is a picture of
the first reading machine. It cost about $20,000. It's about the-- it could scan
a printed page from any book, read it out loud, no
matter what font it was in, and it became
standard in libraries. I've been collaborating with
the National Federation of Blind ever since. For 51 years have actually
been to their convention 51 times, which is not a
record, but it's close to it. The reading machine
is now evolved into a free app on your phone. So it actually is on
your phone, it's free, and you just point it up and you
can read any printed document. It has truly been one of
the most gratifying projects of my career. Being blind doesn't
mean being limited. When we have the
right approaches, the possibilities are endless. And the same is true
for any challenge. With the right ideas and
technology, we can go beyond it. What defines us is our
intelligence, creativity, and passion. And technology helps bring
those qualities forward. So in 1970-- [APPLAUSE] So in 1976, I announced
the reading machine on The Today Show on NBC. We only had one
working prototype and just two hours before we
were scheduled to go live on TV, the machine stopped working. So we frantically
disassembled it backstage, spreading all the
parts across the floor. The person who was
actually due to interview me walked by and said,
is there a problem? [LAUGHTER] No, [INAUDIBLE] said, I'm just
making some final adjustments. So when we reassembled
it, it still didn't work. That's when my chief engineer
lifted the machine up and slammed it down on the
table and, miraculously, it started working. We had 15 minutes to spare
and the demonstration went without a hitch. As it turned out, Stevie Wonder
saw that Today Show segment. He called our office and said,
hi, this is Stevie Wonder. Can I speak to Ray Kurzweil? My receptionist was
skeptical and she said, OK, but I'm Marilyn Monroe. [APPLAUSE] So Stevie arrived
by taxi, he offered to buy the machine, of course,
we wouldn't accept his money. We spent a few hours
showing him how to use it, discussing the
technology behind it, and that was the beginning of
our friendship for the next 50 years. We stayed in touch
over the years, visiting each other whenever
we were on the same Coast. Stevie was actually
quite tech savvy. He was up on all the tech news. And one conversation kept
coming up again and again, which was music, which was
appropriate for this hall. He had just moved
into a new studio. He called it Wonderland. And he told me he was frustrated
that traditional instruments made beautiful sounds, but
no one could play them all. Even if you could play
one, most instruments could only play
one note at a time. Meanwhile, synthesizers gave
musicians a new kind of freedom. You could layer
sounds, you could create complex compositions, you
could create a whole orchestra, but they didn't sound
real instruments. They sounded like organs. So Stevie asked me, would
it be possible to make a synthesizer that
sounds real instruments and have the flexibility
to record and layer music at the same time? I thought it could
be done if we use pattern recognition to create
the nuances of each instrument's sound. The piano was the toughest
because it makes a wide range of complex inharmonic sounds. Most instruments make
vibrations that line up in neat multiples of the main
note, so the piano is different. The extra tones or the
overtones are slightly off tune. That's not true of
other instruments. A little bit out of tune
is what gives the piano its unique sound. Without it, a piano would
sound like an organ. So when we set out to
reproduce it electronically, we had to capture all those
beautiful imperfections. I agreed to pursue
the idea if Stevie would collaborate with us. And so we founded
Kurzweil Music in 1982 with Stevie Wonder
as our advisor. We demoed the prototype of
the Kurzweil 250 in 1983 and we launched it in 1984. It was the first
synthesizer that could truly emulate a piano. Stevie ended up with
about 50 of them. They became central to
the sound of his albums. As I was working on
the Kurzweil keyboard, I began researching the pace
of technological advancement. And I mentioned this earlier,
things are accelerating. As an Inventor, I
learned timing was critical for everything from
inventions, to investments, to romance. You got to be in the right
place at the right time. And even the best
inventions fail if they're released at the wrong time. So, in 1983, I began
tracking the rate of technological progress
to help develop and time my inventions. And that's when I discovered
information technology was advancing at an
exponential rate, before anyone knew
it could happen. You could say I backed
into futurism this way. So this is the
most famous chart. This has been updated
to the present time. I've been updating
this-- actually started when it
was in the middle, but I figured it would
actually continue. Now, as it goes up each
level, it multiplies by 10. So this is actually
an exponential graph. A straight line on
an exponential graph is exponential growth. And it's very much
a straight line. I mean, this is what
actually happened from 1939 to the present. It shows the amount
of computing power you can buy for one
constant dollar. On a logarithmic scale, it
shows a smooth and steady line. And, as I said, this means
consistent exponential growth since the first programmable
computer was released in 1939. Despite major world
events like World War II, economic booms and
busts, regardless of the hardware measured,
this exponential trend goes from relays,
to vacuum tubes, to transistors, to
integrated circuits. This goes way
beyond Moore's law. I mean, there's 80
different charts here. This is the best
computer of that year. Only 10 out of the 80 have
to do with Moore's law. So this goes way
beyond Moore's law. And you can actually make
predictions, which I've done. So in 1939, the first
programmable computer performed 0.000007 calculations per
second per constant dollar. Today, the NVIDIA
B200 chip performs half a trillion calculations
per second per constant dollar. There's a 75
quadrillion-fold increase. That's 75,000 trillion-fold
increase in the amount of computation you can
get for the same money. And that's just the hardware. We're also making
software gains. And the overall
pace of computations equals a hardware
times software. So hardware is 75,000
trillion, we're making about a million-fold
increase in software, so the overall
increase is about 75 million-thousand-trillion-fold
increase in 80 years. That's why we didn't have
large language models in 1939 or even four years ago. People actually introduced large
language models four years ago. They didn't really work. It was kind of a joke. Even a year ago, it wasn't
really didn't work very well. Now it can actually
capture humor and so on. This is the reason why. So as the hardware improves, the
same amount of computing power produces smarter
algorithms, which create better apps, more
powerful tools, bigger breakthroughs. For example, the algorithms
that drive large language models are doubling in efficiency
every eight months. With advances in hardware
and software, the power of AI is growing 10 times per
year for the same costs. We see this exponential
trend specifically with information technologies. We always use the
latest computer chip to develop the next chip. People don't really think
of exponential growth. They think in linear growth. So if something took maybe
10 years in the past, they assume it's going to
take 10 years in the future. That's linear growth. And people anticipate
things will be linear. If you count linear 1, 2, 3,
4, by the time you get to 30, you're at 30. But what's really happening,
you're going 2, 4, 8, 16-- by the time you get to
30, you're at a billion. And that's what's
really happening. So in 1999, I used
this data to predict that computers would reach
artificial general intelligence by 2029. Stanford University had been
looking at my predictions. They were so concerned, they
held a worldwide conference of AI experts from
around the world, several hundred people came. And about 80% agreed that
AI would reach human level intelligence, so they
were agreeing with me, except they thought it would
take 100 years, not 30. And I've actually been running
into some of the people that were there and
they said, yes, we assumed it would take 100 years,
but it looks like it's 30. Today, consensus has fallen
in line with my prediction. Some say it will happen
sooner, like 2027. I've basically gone from being
an optimist to being a pessimist without ever changing my mind. As AI spreads to
every industry, it's going to leap from the digital
world to the physical world, and it will completely transform
everything we care about. So I'll mention one area. One of the biggest
transformation we're going to see from
AI in the near-term is health and medicine. AI can now simulate billions
of molecules and sort through them in record time. So, for example, at the very
start of the COVID pandemic, Moderna used AI to design
and optimize mRNA sequences. In just two days,
they had the formula for the successful vaccine. Two days. The pace was stunning. It usually takes
five to 10 years. Moderna also relied
on AI to accelerate manufacturing and testing,
which got this out very quickly. Going forward, we'll use
biological simulations to find cures for all diseases
like cancer, Alzheimer, and will replace human
trials with digital trials. So instead of testing things
on humans, which take years, we'll test them simulated. And we won't just test them on a
few hundred, 500 or maybe 1,000, different humans, we'll test
them on a million and we'll do that in a matter of days. It'll be much more accurate
and 1,000 times faster. These incredible
breakthroughs are going to lead to what we call
longevity escape velocity. Right now, as you
live through a year, you use up a year
of your longevity. However, if you're diligent
about health care and nutrition, and I assume that
all of you will be, I estimate you'll get back as
much as four months per year today from breakthroughs
in scientific understanding coming up with new
cures, new treatments. In other words, only
lose about eight months of longevity in the year. And as we apply AI to
medicine, these breakthroughs are going to advance
exponentially. So by roughly 2032, when
you live through a year, you'll get back an entire
year from scientific progress. And beyond that point, you'll
get back more than a year for every year you live. So you'll be going
backwards in time as far as your health is concerned. Longevity escape
velocity will start with people who are diligent
about their health care and nutrition. For kids today, or even
people in their 20s and 30s, it's not going to be
difficult to get there. I also believe that
grandparents like me and the other grandparents in
this room, who are proactive, will get there also. Now, this doesn't guarantee
that you'll live forever. You could still die in
an accident tomorrow. What it means is your risk of
dying won't go up as you age. And AI is also
reducing accidents. Take self-driving cars. Human-operated cars, right now,
kill about 40,000 people a year in the United States. Waymo, which is providing a
quarter million rides per week, and that number is
growing exponentially, and has caused no deaths at
all, much better than if they were done by humans. But many people are
worried they will have to compete with
AI for survival. But artificial intelligence
is not an alien invasion. It's evolving from within us. It will reflect our humanity. We're creating it with
values, knowledge, beliefs. From the first hominid
who picked up a stick to reach a higher branch
to Gemini and GPT-5 today, our technology is how we
extend the reach of our bodies and minds. As we move forward, the lines
between humans and technology will blur until we are
ultimately one and the same. The amount of
computation it took up an entire floor
of a building now fits on a smartphone
in your pocket. And what now fits in your pocket
will fit inside a blood cell. We'll be far more powerful. This is how we're
going to merge with AI. In the 2030s, robots the
size of molecules will go into our brains, noninvasively,
through the capillaries and will connect our brains
directly to the cloud. Think of it like having a
phone but in your brain. If you ask or just
think about a question, your brain will go
out to the cloud for an answer like the
way you do on your phone now, only it will be instant. There won't be any
input or output issues. You won't even realize
it's been done. The answer will just
appear in your brain like it's part of you. You won't be able to tell the
difference between ideas that emerge in your biological brain
versus your computational brain. It will be one brain. By 2045, once we have
fully merged with AI, our intelligence will
no longer be constrained by the size of our skulls. It will expand a million fold. It will be more creative,
funny, sexy, compassionate. This is what we call
the singularity. Imagine a world where
AI can collaborate with human artists,
not just as a tool, but as a genuine partner
in the creative process. This has already begun. When my father wanted to
hear his compositions, he had to hire an
entire orchestra to play them, which was
expensive, time-consuming. If he were alive
today, he'd simply play them with a software
program in seconds at no cost. Human creativity is limited
to our understanding of several paths to innovation,
whereas AI's creativity stems from every possible path
to every possible innovation. Even when there are
trillions of possibilities AI will allow us to sort
through all combinations of data to find all possible creative
solutions, which lead us to New innovations at record speed. When we connect our
brains to the cloud, we will not only
expand our memories and speed up our thinking, we'll
add more complex and abstract thinking that we can
even imagine today. It's like asking a mouse
what it's like to be a human. The mouse doesn't understand the
question, let alone the answer, but it's good that we've gone
beyond what a mouse can do. AI is how we humans will
now enhance our education and insight. What's happened to
creativity and skills that are unimaginable today,
like visualizing shapes in 11 dimensions or speaking
every language, which I can already do. We'll have the
capability to recreate ourselves and our world. Remember what happened
2 million years ago, the last time we
gained more neocortex? We became humans. When we can access additional
neocortex in the cloud, the leap in cognitive
abstraction will be similar. The results will
be the invention of new means of expression
vastly richer than the art and technology that
is possible today. The technology has always
been a double-edged sword. The same drone that delivers
medication to a remote hospital can also carry explosives. The threats of AI are real,
must be taken seriously. I think we are doing that. However, it's essential
to also recognize the profound benefits
of AI in order to bring about a
positive future. Optimism is not an
idle speculation. It's a self-fulfilling prophecy. If we assume everything
is getting worse, it will. If you think we are not
making any progress, it's easy to decide
it's not worth trying. We have a moral
imperative to realize the promise of new technologies
while controlling the peril. Both good and bad people
are going to merge with AI and have a sharp
increase in intelligence. We need to protect and
improve our human governance, and social institutions,
and continue to advance our
ethical ideals, which have profoundly reduced
violence, if you look at the data in recent
centuries and decades. When I was growing
up, most people assumed that nuclear
war was inevitable. The fact that our
species found the wisdom to refrain from using these
terrible weapons for the past 80 years shines as an example of
how we have it in our power to use emerging
biotechnology, nanotechnology, superintelligent AI responsibly. We are not doomed to fail
in controlling these risks. As the power of AI
grows, its ability to thwart the misuse of its
capabilities will also grow. I'm very proud that
I've had the opportunity to get to know MIT in
many different ways. I'm confident that this will
continue and grow in the times ahead. Thank you very much. [APPLAUSE] Ray, when I grow up, I
want to be just like you. [LAUGHTER] We have time for
a few questions. Someone will be walking
around with a microphone, so if you have a
question, raise your hand and a microphone
will come to you. Oh, maybe. Maybe I'm the person
with microphones. Thank you, sir. It's delightful to hear
you and your analysis. I'm curious, in both books
envision bots, little nanobots, coursing around our
body, especially understanding our mind. But I also see, I
think, that bots are-- put the fear of invasion of
alien creatures or something. And it's not
happening as broadly as a tool for advancing our
understanding of the brain as I was hoping reading your
first book 100 years ago. Can you comment? Well, right now, I
believe almost everybody has one of these. That was actually
controversial 10 years ago. These existed, but most
people didn't have them. Aaron had one in his car. Now everybody has them. The next step is going
to be virtual reality. You just put on these glasses
and you'll actually see-- you could see a
computer screen or you could see anything you want. And it will actually be-- this is controversial,
but it will happen. It will actually listen
to what you're saying. You're wondering who's
the person in this movie. It will just hear that
and it will pop up. You won't have to
actually ask it. It'll be constantly looking
at it and see what you see. It'll tell you
stories behind it. So your AI will be constantly
addressing what you do. Ultimately, we'll
go inside the mind. There are a few projects
that are doing this today. It will ultimately be
done without surgery. You think that
people won't do it, but the people that actually do
it are going to be far ahead. It's going to be interested
in everything you're doing, issues come up. You're not actually going
to be able to be aware, as I said in my
speech, whether it's coming from your biological mind
or from your computational mind is just come. I mean, you're trying
to think of something and suddenly you're
thinking of it. You don't where that came from. So it will come from
either AI or otherwise. This is already giving us
access to basically everything that human beings
have done already. It's a little bit of-- takes a certain amount
of time to access it. Ultimately, it will
be very instantaneous and there are many different
ways of doing that. But that will
happen in the 2030s. So by 2045, we'll have
multiplied our intelligence a billion-fold, and you
won't really be able to-- I'm sorry, a million-fold. You won't really be able to
tell where it's coming from. Thank you for the very
insightful lecture. I was wondering what
your advice might be to folks who are
still in college or have recently graduated,
how they should position their learning, their
advancement, their career, their work, their
life's goals in the face of this exponential
growth that we're seeing and the transformations
that we should expect in the next few years? I mean, this is a big
issue for education as I interact with different
educational institutions, including for our grandchildren,
like from kindergarten up through graduate school. The idea is, OK,
let's not use AI. AI is interfering
with education. Let's not use it. But the world that
people are being-- are going to graduate
in are already using it. The kids how to use it. They're just not
using it in school. But this is beginning to change. But AI is actually part of are-- of what we know. I mean, AI didn't
come from Mars. It's not some alien invasion
of intelligent beings from some other planet. It actually has everything
that human beings have. And it actually puts things
together in the same way. And we can actually explore and
this is happening right now. We're using AI
simulations of physics to simulate what's going
to happen in medicine, and it's getting
faster and faster. We're actually
putting together a way to simulate all different
types of biological things that happen. I'm talking to people
that are doing this. they feel it'll be completely
finished in about five years. So, I mean, in five
years, we won't really need to experiment on humans. We'll be able to experiment
with simulated biology, which will happen very, very quickly. So we really need
to prepare people to understand this is the
world that we're living in. And it's a good
thing we're actually to be able to master
things much more quickly. So I think it's actually good
to be a young person now. It will actually be somewhat
difficult in that we've got to really change our economy
to be able to master this. But, ultimately, it's going
to give everybody an ability to understand what
everybody has done and access to that
in our own mind. I just wanted to express
profound gratitude for optimism-- hope and optimism, which we
really, really need right now. I brought a group of students
from an international boarding school. So there they are high school
students and one of them has a question. But my question
was, how do we teach the teachers to be
optimistic and especially with the intersection
of AI and creativity, which you've alluded to
several times, but my student-- Well, this is a big issue. There's a desire that people's
biological mind learn things and not just rely on AI. It's hard to do, but that's the
way the educational system has been created. The educational system
moves very slowly. It really was designed
50 or 100 years ago. It's very hard to change it. But the kids are
learning how to use AI. Our grandson, for example,
creates movies using AI. He's 10 and so on. And I don't have all
the answers to that. But we have to be
able to teach them for the world that's going
to exist when they graduate. So we have time for one
more quick question. How about you? Thank you very much. You just made a
comment regarding changing how our economy works. I was wondering if
you could elaborate on that, in light of changes
coming to AI, how we can-- there's going to be some
distributional impacts. Just want to hear your thoughts
on how the economy can be changed or should be changed. Well, the availability of
jobs is going to change. And we're going to be able to
actually know lots of things. So going to one person and
only this very small group can for example code
computers is going to change. We will have-- it's
quite clear we're going to have to give a
certain amount of money to everybody to be able to live. And they'll be able to then
create things that are-- I mean, we've had this before. If you go back 200 years
ago, 80% of our people worked in farming. Today there's 1.4%
and they actually create a lot more food than
the 80% did 200 years ago. Jobs have actually gone up. And lots of things have been-- no, people can't get
jobs in them anymore, but there's new types of jobs
that didn't exist before. So don't worry about
losing your job, you'll be able to get
a job as an influencer and social networks and
people would have no idea what you're talking about. That's going to continue,
but we're actually going to use AI as
part of who we are. It's not going to be us. I've got my intelligence
from my biology and I've got this
AI that's different. It's all going to
be merged together. And we're going to
be able to do things that we couldn't do before. So we're going to have to
make some certain changes so that everybody is happy and
that will create some concern. But the end result of
it will be positive. Thank you very much. Ray, thank you so, so much. [APPLAUSE]

Txz-1lRAWG8.transcript: Ray Kurzweil is a Pioneer in the field of artificial intelligence and a leading futureist who has made some astounding predictions about the future of technology Kurzweil was born in 1948 in New York City and has always been fascinated by the power of technology to change the world he received a bachelor's degree in computer science and literature from MIT and went on to work as an inventor entrepreneur and writer before we continue we wanted to remind you to hit that Thumbs Up Button And subscribe to our Channel if you haven't already that way you won't miss out on any future videos about futurists like Ray Kurzweil or other exciting topics throughout his career Kurzweil has been at the Forefront of some of the most exciting developments in technology he has developed numerous groundbreaking Technologies including the first CCD flatbed scanner the first omnifont optical character recognition software and the first print-to-speech reading machine for the blind but Kurzweil is perhaps best known for his predictions about the future of technology he is famously predicted that by the year 2029 computers will be able to surpass human intelligence and that by 2045 humans and machines will merge to form a singularity a moment of unprecedented technological change kurzweil's predictions have garnered both praise and skepticism but there is no denying the impact that he has had on the field of artificial intelligence his work has inspired a generation of scientists and technologists to push the boundaries of what is possible and to imagine a future where technology and Humanity are inseparable the term Singularity refers to a hypothetical future moment in time when technological progress will accelerate at such a rate that Humanity will be irreversibly transformed Kurzweil believes that this transformation will be triggered by the emergence of artificial intelligence that surpasses human intelligence leading to a rapid acceleration and technological development and emerging of humans and machines kurzweil's concept of the singularity is based on the idea that as technology becomes more advanced it will be able to create even more advanced technology at an accelerating Pace this will lead to a point where technological progress becomes exponential rather than linear and the rate of change becomes almost incomprehensible to the human mind at this point Kurzweil believes that Humanity will be irreversibly transformed and that we will be able to solve some of the most pressing problems facing our world including poverty disease and environmental degradation however he also acknowledges that the singularity could have negative consequences such as the loss of jobs to automation or the potential for artificial intelligence to become a threat to humanity thanks for joining us for this video about futurist Ray Kurzweil we hope you found it informative and thought-provoking if you have any thoughts or opinions about kurzweil's ideas or about the future of technology in general we'd love to hear them in the comments below so don't be shy let us know what you think
